items.name: Overlap
items.blurb: The fullback overlaps outside the winger, forcing the defender to track the run or leave the cross open.
items.when_to_use: Best when the opponent defends narrow and the winger naturally wants to cut inside onto his stronger side.
coaching_points_json: The threat of the run alone drags the defender, even when the ball is never passed.
The winger should delay one beat to let the run mature before deciding.
items.youth_takeaway: Even an unused run can create space: the fullback's sprint alone forces a defensive decision.

steps.caption: Winger receives wide and faces up the fullback.
Own fullback sprints an arced run outside him.
Ball is released into the overlap for the cross or cutback.

items.name: Underlap
items.blurb: A fullback or advanced eight underlaps inside the winger into the half-space between fullback and centre back.
items.when_to_use: Fits when the opponent's winger tracks the outside run, leaving the inside channel open because the touchline winger has stretched them.
coaching_points_json: The touchline winger must hold true width, since that width is what opens the inside lane.
Time the underlap run to arrive as the winger's touch draws the marker's eyes.
items.youth_takeaway: Width and the underlap are a pair: nobody creates the inside lane by running into it alone.

steps.caption: Winger holds the ball high and wide, pinning the opposition fullback to the touchline.
Runner attacks the inside channel between fullback and centre back.
A slipped through-ball sets up the cutback or shot.

items.name: One-Two (Wall Pass / Give-and-Go)
items.blurb: A player passes into a teammate's feet, sprints beyond his marker, and receives the return first time.
items.when_to_use: Suited to tight central areas around the box against opponents marking man to man, since the marker follows the pass and the run beats him.
coaching_points_json: The pass into the wall must be firm and played to the correct foot.
The run starts as the pass travels, not after it arrives.
items.youth_takeaway: The oldest trick in football still works: pass, sprint, receive, because the marker cannot watch the ball and the run at once.

steps.caption: Player A passes into the wall player's feet.
A immediately bursts beyond his marker.
The wall plays a one-touch return into A's path.

items.name: Playing Off the Target: Up-Back (Bounce & Recycle)
items.blurb: A pass up into a striker with his back to goal is set back first time to a facing teammate.
items.when_to_use: Useful when the opposition midfield is compact and cannot be played through directly, so the team goes over it and off the nine instead.
coaching_points_json: The bounce player only needs to shield the ball for one touch, not win the duel.
The support runner arrives at an angle, never flat behind the ball.
items.youth_takeaway: A pass forward can still be a pass back: bouncing it off a teammate keeps the ball moving toward goal.

steps.caption: Vertical pass into the nine's feet, with a centre back tight behind him.
One-touch layoff back to an arriving midfielder.
That midfielder plays forward, or switches the play.

items.name: Third-Man Run (Up-Back-Through)
items.blurb: While everyone watches the first pass, a third player is already running and gets released by the second player's touch.
items.when_to_use: The single most important pattern against an organised defence, and core to every good build-out.
coaching_points_json: Timing beats speed: the runner curves his run to stay onside.
The back pass is what unlocks the run, since it pulls opposition eyes toward the ball.
items.youth_takeaway: The best runs are never on the ball: the third man was never marked because he was never in possession.

steps.caption: The six plays up into the nine or ten between the lines, and the third man's run begins.
Layoff back to the arriving eight.
The eight plays first time through for the third man attacking in behind.

items.name: Switch of Play
items.blurb: After the block shifts to the ball side, one long diagonal finds the free winger on the far side.
items.when_to_use: The opponent defends in a compact ball-side block, the standard plan for teams with an elite one-against-one winger.
coaching_points_json: The switch must arrive before the block slides across, since a slow diagonal wastes the overload.
Build the overload on purpose: three or four deliberate passes on one side before the switch.
items.youth_takeaway: Moving the ball quickly from side to side outruns eleven defenders trying to shift with it.

steps.caption: A deliberate three or four pass overload on the left draws the block across.
The six or centre back receives facing forward.
A forty-yard diagonal finds the right winger in acres of space.
Immediate one-against-one, or a cross.

items.name: Build-Out From the Back (vs press)
items.blurb: Short structured passing from the goalkeeper through the thirds baits the press forward, then plays through or past it.
items.when_to_use: The default restart pattern for possession identities, whenever the opponent presses with its front line.
coaching_points_json: The press is an invitation, not a problem: every presser who jumps leaves a free man behind him.
Know where the spare man is before the ball arrives, not after.
items.youth_takeaway: Passing calmly under pressure is a skill you can drill: know your free man before the ball comes to you.

steps.caption: The keeper splits the centre backs wide while the six shows between them.
The opposition strikers jump, and a free man appears behind them.
A line-broken pass finds the eight or ten between the lines.
Runners are released, continuing into a third-man combination.

items.name: Attracting Pressure to Release: La Pausa (Waiting for Pressure)
items.blurb: A player on the ball waits, invites the presser to commit, and releases the pass only as he does.
items.when_to_use: A foundation skill for possession-based identities, and the individual twin of team-level build-out play.
coaching_points_json: Passing before pressure arrives wastes the ball's power, since nothing about the defence was displaced.
The sequence is receive, invite, wait for the commitment, then release.
items.youth_takeaway: Holding the ball on purpose is not hesitation: it is what makes the pass that follows unstoppable.

steps.caption: The centre back or six receives with time and deliberately holds the ball, exposed as bait.
The presser commits, his weight going forward past the point of no return.
The ball is released past him into the line-break target, and the presser is now behind the play.

items.name: Press Baiting (Team-Level Traps in Possession)
items.blurb: The team plays deliberately into a trapped area to draw the opponent's press forward and out of shape.
items.when_to_use: Against aggressive pressing teams, whose own aggression becomes the raw material for the trap.
coaching_points_json: Every bait needs its exit planned before it is played: know the out-ball and where the second ball lands.
A backward pass is a forward play: the ball goes back so the team can go forward.
items.youth_takeaway: Sometimes passing backward is the bravest attacking decision on the pitch, because it sets the trap for what comes next.

steps.caption: The ball goes backward to the keeper, and the opposition line pushes up in response.
A pass to the pressing-trap fullback draws their winger and eight to converge.
The prepared out-ball clips over the jumped press into the space the block abandoned.

items.name: Counter-Attack (Vertical Transition)
items.blurb: Win the ball, then attack the disorganised opponent before it can reform, ideally finishing inside ten seconds.
items.when_to_use: A core weapon of counter-attacking and hybrid identities, and situationally available to any team.
coaching_points_json: The first two seconds decide the counter: face forward and play forward.
If the vertical pass is not on by the second pass, keep the ball and reorganise instead.
items.youth_takeaway: Winning the ball is only half the job: the team must already know who sprints and where.

steps.caption: The ball is regained in the team's own half.
The first pass goes forward into the ten or wide forward.
Two runners break beyond the ball at full sprint into the channels and the far post.
The move finishes before the opponent's recovery lines can form.

items.name: Long Ball & The Second-Ball Game
items.blurb: The team skips the build-up to hit the target man, then swarms to win the second ball that follows.
items.when_to_use: Against high presses, on difficult pitches, or as a deliberate identity built around territory.
coaching_points_json: A long ball is not a hopeful ball: the target and the cage around him are pre-planned.
Compactness around the landing zone decides the whole contest.
items.youth_takeaway: Football's second contest matters as much as the first: winning the loose ball is a team job, not a lucky bounce.

steps.caption: The keeper or centre back launches long to the target man against a marking centre back.
A pre-set second-ball cage of midfielders and wide players is already in position before the ball lands.
The second ball is won and played forward immediately.

items.name: Pressing Triggers
items.blurb: A coordinated press is released by specific cues, such as a back pass, a bad touch, or a rolling ball.
items.when_to_use: Teachable to any identity, with the most aggressive version defining gegenpressing teams.
coaching_points_json: Press the pass, not the man: arrive as the ball arrives.
One player pressing alone is worse than nobody pressing at all.
items.youth_takeaway: A good press is not about running fast, it is about running at the right moment.

steps.caption: The team holds a mid-block shape in a patient phase.
A trigger event flashes, such as a back pass to the centre back.
The whole unit jumps together: the nearest player curves his run to cut the return pass.
